title: Complex Numbers
blurb: The number i, arithmetic with a + bi, conjugates and division, and quadratics whose roots finally all exist.

lessons.title: Meet i
lessons.concreteComparison: Negatives were once 'absurd' (what's minus three sheep?). Irrationals scandalized the Greeks. Every number-system upgrade started as heresy and ended as Tuesday. The last upgrade: $x^2 = -1$ has no real answer — so mathematicians INVENTED one, named it $i$, and demanded only that $i^2 = -1$. Everything else about algebra survives untouched.
lessons.objective: Define $i$ by $i^2 = -1$, simplify square roots of negatives, and compute powers of $i$ using the 4-cycle.
concept: **Definition**: $i$ is a new number with one property: $i^2 = -1$. Nothing on the real line squares to a negative, so $i$ lives OFF the line — 'imaginary' is a historical nickname, not an insult.
Roots of negatives factor through $i$: $\sqrt{-9} = \sqrt{9}\sqrt{-1} = 3i$; $\sqrt{-50} = 5i\sqrt{2}$ — pull the $\sqrt{-1}$ out FIRST, then simplify as in the radicals unit.
Powers of $i$ cycle with period 4: $i^1 = i$, $i^2 = -1$, $i^3 = -i$, $i^4 = 1$, then repeat. For $i^{37}$: divide 37 by 4, remainder 1 → $i^{37} = i$.
Old equations gain answers: $x^2 = -25$ now solves — $x = \pm 5i$. Check: $(5i)^2 = 25 i^2 = -25$ ✓.
lessons.keyIdea: i² = −1 and nothing else changes. √(−n) = i√n; powers of i cycle i, −1, −i, 1 — remainder mod 4 decides.

workedExamples.solution: $3i$ and $5i\sqrt{2}$ — pull out $i$, then simplify the radical.

commonMistakes.correction: $\sqrt{-4}\sqrt{-9} = (2i)(3i) = 6i^2 = -6$ — NOT $\sqrt{36} = 6$. Convert to $i$-form FIRST; the product rule only held for nonnegatives.

commonMistakes.text: Treating $i$ like a variable that can equal anything.
commonMistakes.correction: $i$ is a fixed CONSTANT (like π), pinned by $i^2 = -1$. It never 'takes values' — it is one.

steps.title: Numbers keep growing
beats: Every extension was scandal → tool → Tuesday.
One equation still had no answer: $x^2 = -1$…

steps.title: One rule only
beats: Keep every algebra rule you already own.
'Imaginary' was Descartes mocking it. The name stuck; the mockery didn't.

steps.title: Powers of i
beats: Exponent mod 4 — that's the whole trick.

steps.title: i-arithmetic
steps.intro: Pull out i first; ride the 4-cycle.

problems.explanation: $4i^2 = -4$. Squaring an imaginary lands on the negative reals.
$(2i)(3i) = 6i^2 = -6$. (Merging under one root gives $+6$ — the product rule's revenge.)

steps.title: The engineer's j
steps.body: Electrical engineers write $j$ instead of $i$ (their $i$ was taken — current). Every AC circuit, every phone signal, every power grid is analyzed with complex numbers: impedance is a complex number, and 'imaginary' power is real enough to heat transformers. The 'impossible number' runs the electrical world under an alias.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $i^2 = -1$ — the only new rule; all algebra survives.
Powers of $i$ cycle with period 4; exponent mod 4 decides.

lessons.title: Complex Numbers: a + bi
lessons.concreteComparison: Mixing reals and imaginaries makes a two-part number, like coordinates or '3 dollars and 40 cents': $z = a + bi$ keeps a **real part** and an **imaginary part** that never blend. Adding and subtracting is bookkeeping — reals with reals, imaginaries with imaginaries — the like-terms discipline you've run since Grade 6.
lessons.objective: Identify real and imaginary parts, and add, subtract, and scale complex numbers by combining like parts.
concept: **Complex number**: $z = a + bi$ — real part $a$, imaginary part $b$ (the COEFFICIENT of $i$, a real number). $3 - 7i$: real 3, imaginary $-7$.
Reals and imaginaries are both special cases: $5 = 5 + 0i$ and $2i = 0 + 2i$. The complex numbers CONTAIN everything before them.
**Add/subtract componentwise**: $(2+3i) + (4-5i) = 6 - 2i$; subtraction distributes the minus over BOTH parts.
**Equality is two equations**: $a + bi = c + di$ forces $a = c$ AND $b = d$. Two-part numbers carry two facts — this powers 'find x and y' problems.
lessons.keyIdea: z = a + bi: two parts that never blend. Combine componentwise; equality matches both parts separately.

facts.title: Anatomy
facts.explanation: Real part a; imaginary part b (a real number!).

facts.title: Equality
facts.explanation: One complex equation = two real ones.

workedExamples.solution: Sum: $6 - 2i$. Difference: $-2 + 8i$ (the minus hits both parts).

commonMistakes.text: Blending the parts: $(2+3i) + (4-5i) = 4i$ or similar mashups.
commonMistakes.correction: Reals never merge with imaginaries — they're different species, like meters and seconds. $6$ and $-2i$ stay separate: $6 - 2i$.
The imaginary PART is the real coefficient $-7$. (Convention matters when matching parts in equations.)

steps.title: Dollars and cents
beats: $z = a + bi$: a REAL part and an IMAGINARY part.
Like '3 dollars 40 cents' — two components, one number.

steps.title: The family tree
beats: The Grade-8 number-system diagram grew a new ring.

steps.title: Componentwise
steps.explanation: Reals: $7-3 = 4$. Imaginaries: $-2+9 = 7$. Two ledgers, no blending.

steps.title: Distribute the minus
beats: The Grade-10 minus trap, now in two dimensions.

steps: One complex equation, two real answers.

problems.explanation: The part is the real COEFFICIENT of $i$: $-11$.
Reals cancel; imaginaries double: $8i$ — a purely imaginary result.
Zero has both parts zero — matching parts gives two equations, both forced.

steps.title: The name that stuck
steps.body: Gauss hated 'imaginary' and proposed 'lateral numbers' — sideways from the real line, which is exactly right (next lessons make it literal). He lost the naming battle but won the math: the complex numbers' full acceptance traces to his 1799 proof that every polynomial equation has solutions in them. Names lie; theorems don't.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $z = a + bi$: real and imaginary parts, forever separate.
Add/subtract componentwise; minus hits both parts.
Matching parts turns one complex equation into two real ones.

lessons.title: Multiplying Complex Numbers
lessons.concreteComparison: You already own the tool: FOIL from Grade 10. $(1+2i)(3-i)$ expands exactly like $(1+2x)(3-x)$ — four products, collect terms — with ONE twist at the end: any $i^2$ that appears becomes $-1$ and jumps ledgers, from the imaginary column into the real one. Old machine, one new gear.
lessons.objective: Multiply complex numbers by expanding and converting $i^2 = -1$, including squares and the special conjugate product.
concept: FOIL, then convert: $(1+2i)(3-i) = 3 - i + 6i - 2i^2 = 3 + 5i + 2 = 5 + 5i$. The $-2i^2$ became $+2$ — real.
Squares work the same: $(3+2i)^2 = 9 + 12i + 4i^2 = 5 + 12i$. (The perfect-square pattern from Grade 10, plus the conversion.)
The star product: $(a+bi)(a-bi) = a^2 + b^2$ — REAL and positive. The imaginary cross-terms cancel and $-b^2i^2 = +b^2$. This 'conjugate pair' powers next lesson's division.
Multiplying by $i$ alone: $i(a+bi) = -b + ai$ — the parts SWAP (with a sign). Geometrically that's a quarter-turn, a teaser for the complex plane.
lessons.keyIdea: Expand like polynomials, then i² → −1 (jumping to the real ledger). Conjugate pairs multiply to a² + b², real.

facts.title: The routine
facts.explanation: One new gear on the old machine.

commonMistakes.correction: $3 + 5i - 2i^2$ isn't finished — convert: $-2i^2 = +2$, giving $5 + 5i$. Sweep every $i^2$ into the real ledger before boxing the answer.
Squares have a MIDDLE term: $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$ — the $12i$ survives. Grade 10's missing-middle error, complex edition.

steps.title: Where i² goes
beats: Every $i^2$ is secretly a REAL number: $-1$.
So $ki^2$ leaves the imaginary column as $-k$.
An answer still holding $i^2$ isn't finished.
Final form: exactly $a + bi$, both parts clean.

steps.title: Conjugates annihilate i
beats: Radicals had this trick too — next lesson weaponizes it.

steps.title: Product lines
steps.intro: FOIL, convert, collect — and the star.

steps: Powers of complex numbers: square, then square again.

examples.prompt: Multiply $i(5 - 2i)$ and describe what happened to the parts.
steps: Parts swapped (with a sign): $(5, -2) \to (2, 5)$ — a quarter-turn.

problems.explanation: $1 + 2i + i^2 = 2i$ — the middle term IS the answer. (Dropping it gives 0: the classic.)

steps.title: Fractals are complex multiplication
steps.body: The Mandelbrot set — the most famous image in mathematics — is drawn by repeatedly squaring complex numbers: $z \to z^2 + c$, over and over, coloring points by whether they escape to infinity. Every psychedelic zoom video you've seen is $(a+bi)^2$, iterated billions of times. This lesson's FOIL is the fractal's entire engine.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Multiply like polynomials; then $i^2 \to -1$ jumps ledgers.
$(a+bi)(a-bi) = a^2 + b^2$ — the real-making star product.

lessons.title: Conjugates & Division
lessons.concreteComparison: Radicals taught you to clean $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt3 - 1}$ with a conjugate. Complex fractions have the identical problem — $\tfrac{3+i}{1-i}$ has an 'unreal' denominator — and the identical cure: multiply top and bottom by the **conjugate** $1+i$, and the bottom becomes the star product $a^2 + b^2$: a plain positive real. Same trick, new number system.
lessons.objective: Use the conjugate $\bar{z} = a - bi$ and modulus to divide complex numbers, landing answers in clean $a + bi$ form.
concept: **Conjugate**: $\overline{a+bi} = a - bi$ — flip the imaginary sign. Key identity: $z\bar{z} = a^2 + b^2$, always real, always $\ge 0$.
**Modulus**: $|z| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}$ — the number's 'size'. $|3+4i| = 5$. So $z\bar z = |z|^2$.
**Division**: multiply top and bottom by the bottom's conjugate. $\tfrac{3+i}{1-i} = \tfrac{(3+i)(1+i)}{(1-i)(1+i)} = \tfrac{2+4i}{2} = 1 + 2i$.
Sanity check divisions by multiplying back: $(1+2i)(1-i) = 3 + i$ ✓. Division is defined by what multiplication undoes.
lessons.keyIdea: Conjugate flips the i-sign; z·z̄ = a² + b² = |z|². Divide by multiplying with the bottom's conjugate.

facts.title: Division
facts.explanation: Bottom goes real; then split the parts.

commonMistakes.text: Conjugating BOTH the top and the bottom.
commonMistakes.correction: Multiply top and bottom by the BOTTOM's conjugate (×1 in disguise). Conjugating the numerator too changes the value — that's a different number.
$|3-4i| = \sqrt{9+16} = 5$, not $\sqrt{9-16}$. The modulus squares both parts and ADDS — Pythagoras, as the next lesson makes literal.

steps.title: Conjugate and modulus
beats: $z\bar z = a^2+b^2$ — the star product from last lesson.
So $z\bar z = |z|^2$. Three ideas, one identity.

steps.title: Conjugate service
steps.intro: Bottom's conjugate; verify by multiplying back.

steps.title: Division fluency
steps.intro: The bottom's conjugate is the whole trick.

problems.explanation: Only the i-sign flips; the real part stays put.
Real denominators just divide both parts — no conjugate needed.

steps.title: Conjugates catch errors for free
steps.body: Because real-coefficient math treats $i$ and $-i$ identically, conjugating every number in a correct complex calculation yields another correct calculation. Engineers exploit this as a checksum: run the computation, conjugate, run again — disagreement means a bug. The mirror symmetry of $\pm i$ is a built-in proofreader.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $\bar z$ flips the i-sign; $z\bar z = |z|^2 = a^2 + b^2$.
Divide by the bottom's conjugate (×1 in disguise).
Verify every division by multiplying back.

lessons.title: Complex Roots of Quadratics
lessons.concreteComparison: Grade 10 left a case marked 'no solution': the discriminant $D < 0$, the parabola floating clear of the axis. Reopen the file with $i$ in hand — $\sqrt{-16} = 4i$ — and the quadratic formula finishes what it started: $x^2 + 2x + 5 = 0$ gives $x = -1 \pm 2i$. EVERY quadratic now has exactly two roots (counting the double). Case closed.
lessons.objective: Solve quadratics with negative discriminants, getting conjugate-pair roots, and verify them by substitution.
concept: The formula never changes: $x = \tfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{D}}{2a}$. When $D < 0$, write $\sqrt{D} = i\sqrt{|D|}$ and keep going.
Complex roots of real quadratics arrive in **conjugate pairs**: if $-1+2i$ is a root, $-1-2i$ must be too. (The $\pm$ builds the pair; the parabola misses the axis symmetrically.)

Verify like always — substitute: $(-1+2i)^2 + 2(-1+2i) + 5 = (-3-4i) + (-2+4i) + 5 = 0$ ✓. And the full discriminant story completes: $D > 0$ two real, $D = 0$ one real (double), $D < 0$ two complex conjugates.
lessons.keyIdea: D < 0: √D = i√|D| and the formula continues. Roots come in conjugate pairs; every quadratic has exactly 2 roots.

workedExamples.statement: Build the quadratic (leading 1) whose roots are $3 \pm i$.

commonMistakes.correction: No REAL solution — but two complex ones. The right answer now names them: $x = -1 \pm 2i$. 'No solution' retired the day $i$ arrived.

commonMistakes.text: Losing the ÷2a on the imaginary part.
commonMistakes.correction: $x = \tfrac{-2 \pm 4i}{2}$: the 2 divides BOTH terms → $-1 \pm 2i$, not $-1 \pm 4i$. Same whole-top rule as Grade 10.

tryIt.statement: One root of a real quadratic is $5 - 2i$. The other is…

steps.title: Reopening 'no solution'
beats: Grade 10, discriminant $-16$: 'no real solution.' File closed.
The formula was never broken — just blocked.
Unblock it and finish the case.

steps.title: x² + 2x + 5 = 0
beats: The ÷2 hits BOTH terms — old rule, still law.

steps.title: Conjugates or nothing
beats: Real quadratic, complex roots → always $p \pm qi$.
The $\pm$ in the formula BUILDS the pair.
Know one root? The other is free — flip the i-sign.
(Sum $2p$ and product $p^2+q^2$: both real — as they must be.)

steps.title: Use the pair law
steps.prompt: A real quadratic has root $4 + 3i$. Its other root is…
steps.explanation: Conjugate pair: flip only the i-sign. (Sum 8, product 25 — the quadratic is $x^2 - 8x + 25$.)

steps.title: Case files
steps.intro: D, then i√|D|, then divide the whole top.

examples.answer: two real / one double / conjugate pair

steps.title: Full-spectrum solving
steps.intro: Name the roots — 'no solution' is retired.

problems.prompt: Counting multiplicity and complex roots, EVERY quadratic has…
problems.explanation: The discriminant's three cases all deliver two: distinct real, double real, or a conjugate pair. Algebra's books finally balance.

steps.title: The theorem this completes
steps.body: Gauss's Fundamental Theorem of Algebra: every degree-n polynomial has exactly n roots in the complex numbers. The polynomial unit's 'at most n' was the real-line shadow; with $i$, 'at most' becomes 'exactly'. No further number-inventions needed — the complex numbers are algebraically CLOSED. The tower of heresies is finished.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Complex roots of real quadratics: conjugate pairs, always.
Every quadratic: exactly two roots. The books balance.

lessons.title: The Complex Plane
lessons.concreteComparison: Where does $3 + 4i$ LIVE? Not on the number line — it's two-dimensional. Put the reals on a horizontal axis, the imaginaries on a vertical one, and $a + bi$ becomes the POINT $(a, b)$: Gauss's 'lateral numbers', literally. The modulus becomes a distance, conjugates become mirror images, and multiplying by $i$ becomes a quarter-turn — arithmetic turns into geometry.
lessons.objective: Plot complex numbers as points, read modulus as distance from the origin, and see conjugation and multiplication-by-i geometrically.
concept: **The plane**: horizontal axis = real part, vertical = imaginary part. $3+4i \leftrightarrow (3,4)$; reals live on the x-axis, pure imaginaries on the y-axis.
**Modulus = distance**: $|3+4i| = \sqrt{3^2+4^2} = 5$ — the straight-line distance from the origin. The formula was Pythagoras all along.
**Conjugate = reflection**: $\bar z$ flips the sign of $b$ — the mirror image across the real axis. (Quadratics' conjugate root pairs sit symmetrically about it.)

**Multiplying by $i$ = quarter-turn**: $i(3+4i) = -4+3i$ — the point $(3,4)$ rotates $90°$ counterclockwise to $(-4,3)$. Four multiplications return home: that IS the $i^4 = 1$ cycle. Rotation is why engineers model spinning circuits with complex numbers.
lessons.keyIdea: a + bi = the point (a, b): modulus is distance (Pythagoras), conjugation is mirror-flip, ×i is a 90° turn.

workedExamples.statement: Plot-data: where do $3+4i$, $-2+i$, and $-3i$ sit, and how far is each from the origin?
workedExamples.solution: $(3,4)$: dist 5; $(-2,1)$: dist $\sqrt5$; $(0,-3)$: dist 3 (on the imaginary axis).

workedExamples.statement: Rotate $z = 3+4i$ by $90°$ (multiply by $i$), and check the distance survived.
Apply $\times i$ four times to $2 + i$ and watch it come home.

commonMistakes.correction: Real part → HORIZONTAL, imaginary → vertical: $3+4i$ is $(3,4)$, three right and four up. (Alphabetical again: a then b, x then y.)

commonMistakes.text: Expecting $\times i$ to stretch the number.
commonMistakes.correction: $|i| = 1$: multiplying by $i$ only ROTATES — distances survive. Stretching needs $|z| \neq 1$ (e.g. $\times 2i$ turns AND doubles).

tryIt.statement: How far is $-6 + 8i$ from the origin?

steps.title: Gauss's lateral numbers
beats: $3+4i$ can't fit on a LINE — it has two parts.
Give it a PLANE: reals across, imaginaries up.
The 'imaginary' numbers were sideways all along.

steps.title: Distance and mirrors
beats: $|z| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}$: the distance to the origin. Pythagoras!
$\bar z$: flip $b$ — the MIRROR across the real axis.
Conjugate root pairs: symmetric about that mirror.
Division's tricks were geometry in disguise.

steps.title: Locate and measure
steps.prompt: $-5 + 12i$ sits in which quadrant, at what distance?

steps.title: Quarter-turn
options: $-5$ — the point turned onto the negative real axis
steps.explanation: $(0,5)$ turns $90°$ CCW to $(-5,0)$: $i \cdot 5i = 5i^2 = -5$. Top of the plane → left of the plane.

examples.prompt: For $z = 4 - 3i$: plot-data for $z$, $\bar z$, and $iz$, with distances.
steps: All three: distance 5 — mirrors and turns preserve size.

examples.prompt: Which is farther from the origin: $6+2i$ or $4+5i$?

problems.prompt: Which numbers sit exactly distance 1 from the origin?
problems.explanation: The UNIT CIRCLE lives here too: all of $|z| = 1$. (Last topic's circle was the complex plane in disguise.)

problems.prompt: Rotating $7$ (the real number) by two quarter-turns (×$i$ twice) gives…
problems.explanation: $i^2 \cdot 7 = -7$: half a turn lands on the opposite side. 'Multiplying by $-1$ is a 180° rotation' — now literally.

steps.title: The most beautiful equation
steps.body: Push this geometry one step further and you reach $e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0$ — Euler's identity, voted mathematics' most beautiful equation: it says 'rotating by π lands on −1', tying $e$, $i$, $\pi$, 1, and 0 in one line. You now know what three of its five characters are doing there. University finishes the story.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $a+bi$ lives at $(a,b)$: reals across, imaginaries up.
The unit circle returns: $|z| = 1$ is trig's circle, complexified.

practice.solution: Both $53$ — the star product IS the modulus squared.

practice.statement: One root is $2 - 5i$ (real coefficients). The other root, their sum, and product?
Distance of $-9 + 12i$ from the origin, and the effect of ×$i$ on it?
